titleStmt.title: Quasiclassical theory of superconducting spin-splitter effects and spin-filtering via altermagnets

affiliation.orgName: Norwegian University of Science and Technology

analytic.title: Quasiclassical theory of superconducting spin-splitter effects and spin-filtering via altermagnets

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Conducting altermagnets have recently emerged as intriguing materials supporting strongly spin-polarized currents without magnetic stray fields. We demonstrate that altermagnets enable three key functionalities, merging superconductivity and spintronics. The first prediction is a controllable supercurrent-induced edge magnetization, which acts like a dissipationless spin-splitter effect. The second and third predictions are a Cooper pair spin-splitter and a filtering effect, respectively. These effects allow for spatial separation of triplet pairs with opposite spin-polarizations and spin-selective tunneling of Cooper pairs. We derive a quasiclassical theory with associated boundary conditions that describe these phenomena and explain how they can be experimentally verified. Our results open a new path for spatial control of spin signals via triplet Cooper pairs using hybrid superconductor-altermagnet devices.

Introduction.-The interaction between magnetic and superconducting materials is a significant research topic in condensed matter physics [1][2][3]. In addition to revealing new fundamental quantum physics, such material heterostructures are promising in cryogenic technology applications as extremely sensitive detectors of radiation and heat [4] and circuit components for qubits and dissipationless diodes [5].

A recent development in the magnetic community is the discovery of a class of antiferromagnetic materials that feature strongly spin-polarized itinerant electrons despite the absence of a net magnetization. Such materials, known as altermagnets [6], are not invariant under the combined operation of parity and time-reversal and feature a spin-split band structure with a different origin than relativistic effects such as spin-orbit coupling. Because of this, the spin-splitting in altermagnets can be very large, up to the order of an electron volt. Ab initio calculations have identified several possible material candidates that can feature altermagnetism, including metals like RuO 2 and Mn 5 Si 3 as well as semiconductors/insulators like MnF 2 and La 2 CuO 4 [7][8][9][10][11][12]. Very recent experiments have, via photoemission spectroscopy, confirmed the presence of an altermagnetic state in RuO 2 [13], MnTe [14,15] and CrSb [16]. Moreover, single-domain samples of order 20 𝜇m 2 have recently been obtained using MnTe [17], which is large enough [18] to perform transport measurements in layered structures through a single altermagnetic domain.

As for conventional antiferromagnets, altermagnets have no magnetic stray field and exhibit ultrafast THz spin dynamics. Analogous to ferromagnets, the large anisotropic spin-splitting of electron bands renders the spin degree of freedom more accessible. For these reasons, altermagnets have quickly become promising candidates for spintronics. Their unique anisotropic spin-splitting allows for interesting transport phenomena such as spin-polarized currents [19,20], giant-and tunneling magnetoresistance [21], anomalous Hall effect [22], and spin-splitter effect [23][24][25][26].

On the other hand, the anisotropic spin-split bands have dramatic effects on the pairing symmetry of an accompanying superconducting state, intrinsic or in proximity contact with another material. This interplay between superconductivity and altermagnetism has very recently started to be explored, includ- ing the Josephson effect [27][28][29], the superconducting phase transition in the presence of altermagnetism [30][31][32][33][34], magnoninduced superconductivity [35,36], and Andreev reflection [37,38].

The use of altermagnets in the field of superconducting spintronics is, however, still underexplored. Here, we will show that altermagnets strongly influence the transport of spin-polarized Cooper pairs. We predict that altermagnets induce two super-conducting spin-splitter effects, as shown in Fig. 1. First, a supercurrent causes an edge-magnetization in an altermagnet, with a polarization that flips when the supercurrent is reversed. Moreover, Cooper pairs polarized along or opposite to the staggered order in the altermagnet accumulate at opposite edges of the altermagnet. We also show that altermagnets filter Cooper pairs with opposite spins. These phenomena can generate large spin supercurrents and dissipationless magnetoelectric effects without spin-orbit interactions and magnetic stray fields. Hence, we enable new ways to control triplet superconducting correlations spatially, offering a promising direction in the interplay between magnetism and superconductivity. In predicting these phenomena, we develop a quasiclassical theory of superconducting correlations in altermagnets and supplement this framework with boundary conditions between altermagnets and superconductors.

Theory.-We use the quasiclassical theory of superconductivity to describe proximity-coupled altermagnets with superconductors [39]. We describe the spin splitting of the altermagnet through the effective Hamiltonian
div.formula: 𝐻 AM = ∑︁ 𝜎 𝜎 ′ ∫ 𝑑 𝒓𝜓 † 𝜎 (𝒓, 𝑡) 𝛼 2𝑚 [𝝈 ¯• l] 𝜎 𝜎 ′ 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 𝜓 𝜎 ′ (𝒓, 𝑡),(1)
div.p: where 𝜓 𝜎 (𝒓, 𝑡) is the electronic wave function for spin 𝜎 at position 𝒓 and time 𝑡, 𝛼 is a dimensionless parameter specifying the strength of the altermagnetic term, and the canonical momentum is p = -i∇. We set ℏ = 1. Furthermore, l is the Néel vector defined as the difference between the two sublattice magnetizations, and 𝑚 is the electron mass. In this work, matrices in spin space are represented by 𝑎, while matrices in Nambu space, i.e. the 4 × 4 space of spin and particle-hole degrees of freedom, are denoted by â. Finally, ǎ is a matrix in Keldysh-Nambu space, with the appropriate causality structure contingent on whether ǎ is a Green's function, a self-energy, or an inverse of these [40]. The real-space orientation of the altermagnet is captured by the matrix
div.formula: 𝑇 = cos 2𝜃 sin 2𝜃 sin 2𝜃 -cos 2𝜃 ,(2)
div.p: where 𝜃 parametrizes the orientation of the altermagnet as shown in Fig. 1. We emphasize that the angle 𝜃 describes the orientation of the crystal lattice, not the orientation of the magnetic moments. The altermagnetic order effectively modifies the Eilenberger equation [41] to become i We use the gradient approximation in quasiclassical theory and assume the limit of a weak altermagnet with nearly constant Fermi momentum magnitude 𝑝 𝐹 . In this limit, the altermagnet gives rise to the three last terms in the Eilenberger Eq. ( 3). This is our first result. Notably, the two last terms from the altermagnetic order in Eq. ( 3) disallow the conventional normalization condition ǧ2 = 1. Higher-order terms from intrinsic spin-orbit coupling have a similar effect [42]. This prohibits the standard procedure for deriving the Usadel equation, which relies on the above normalization of ǧ. However, this difficulty can be circumvented in the linearized regime, which is relevant for weakly proximity-coupled hybrid structures where the anomalous quasiclassical Green's function is small. At equilibrium, it is sufficient to consider the retarded quasiclassical Green's function. We perform the linearization ĝ𝑅 ≈ ρ3 + f . The offdiagonal part of the deviation f from the normal-state solution captures singlet-and triplet superconducting correlations. The absence of the normalization ǧ2 = 1 also allows for diagonal corrections in f in the quasiclassical Green's function. In the Sup. Mat. [43], we show that these diagonal corrections do not affect the magnetization induced by the superconducting spin-splitter effects predicted here. Hence, we do not consider these corrections in further detail in the main text.
div.formula: 𝑚 p 𝐹 • ∇ R ǧ + [𝐸 ρ3 -Σtot , ǧ] -- 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝑝 𝐹, 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] - + i𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] + + 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] -= 0,(3)
where m = diag [𝝈 • l, [𝝈 • l] * ], ρ3 = diag(1, 1, -1, -
div.p: Without loss of generality, we define the Néel vector l to be along the ẑ direction in spin space. In the dirty limit, we average over the Fermi surface to find the Usadel equation:
div.formula: 𝐷∇ 2 R 𝑓 ¯= -2i𝐸 𝑓 ¯-𝛼𝐷 2 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]+ - i𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]-,(4)
div.p: where 𝜏 ¯3 = diag(1, -1) and the diffusion constant is 𝐷 = 𝜏𝑣 2 𝐹 /2 in terms of the elastic scattering time 𝜏 and the Fermi velocity 𝑣 𝐹 . We underline that the above equation is derived without utilizing any normalization condition, which is possible due to the assumption of a weak proximity effect [42].

The prefactors of the second and third terms on the righthand side of Eq. (4) scale differently with the degree of disorder. The leading order contribution of the altermagnet is captured by the second term on the right-hand side in Eq. ( 4). This term gives rise to Cooper pair spin-filtering for altermagnets oriented with 𝜃 = 0 and a Cooper pair spin-splitter effect for a 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 orientation, as we will show below. The third term on the right-hand side in Eq. ( 4) is smaller by a factor 𝑚𝐷, which is proportional to 𝜆 𝐹 /𝑙, where 𝜆 𝐹 is the Fermi wavelength and 𝑙 is the mean free path. This term gives rise to singlet-triplet mixing.

We derive appropriate tunneling boundary conditions to capture the effects of proximity-coupled superconductors and altermagnets. To this end, we modify the spin-active Kupriyanov-Lukichev (KL) boundary conditions in quasiclassical theory, which are expressed as [44][45][46][47]
div.formula: 2 Ǐ𝑅 = 𝐺 0 [ ǧ𝐿 , ǧ𝑅 ] -+ i𝐺 𝜙 [ ǧ𝑅 , m𝑥 ] -.(5)
div.p: Here, Ǐ𝑅 is the matrix current on the right side of the junction while ǧ𝐿(𝑅) is the quasiclassical Green function on the left (right) side of the boundary, and 𝐺 0 is the tunneling interface transparency. The spin-mixing conductance 𝐺 𝜙 parametrizes interfacial spin polarization in the 𝑥-direction. The matrix current operator is modified for altermagnets, and reads
div.formula: Ǐ𝑅 = 𝐺 𝐿 ǧ𝑅 (∇ 𝑅 𝑖 + 𝛼𝜎 ¯𝑧𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 ) ǧ𝑅 .(6)
div.p: For the interface where the AM is on the left side, the boundary condition is obtained by letting Ǐ𝑅 → -Ǐ𝐿 on the left-hand side and ǧ𝐿 ↔ ǧ𝑅 on the right-hand side of the boundary condition equation. In Eq. ( 6), 𝐺 is the bulk conductance of the altermagnet, and 𝐿 is the material length. The details of the derivation and specific boundary conditions for 𝜃 = 0 and 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 are outlined in the Supp. Mat. [43]. For concreteness, we fix the values of the interface transparency to 𝐺 0 /𝐺 = 0.4 and 𝐺 𝜙 /𝐺 = 0.1. We use a singlet-triplet decomposition 𝑓 = ( 𝑓 𝑠 + d • 𝝈)i𝜎 2 , where 𝑓 𝑠 is the singlet component and the vector d parametrizes the triplet correlations [48]. The linearized Usadel equation forms two sets of coupled differential equations. In terms of the spin-polarized Cooper pair correlations 𝑓 ↑ = i𝑑 𝑦 -𝑑 𝑥 and 𝑓 ↓ = i𝑑 𝑦 + 𝑑 𝑥 , we find
div.formula: 𝐷∇ 2 𝑓 ↑ = -2i𝐸 𝑓 ↑ + 𝛼𝐷𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑓 ↑ (7a) 𝐷∇ 2 𝑓 ↓ = -2i𝐸 𝑓 ↓ -𝛼𝐷𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑓 ↓ .(7b)
div.p: Similarly, the singlet-and triplet correlations are coupled as
div.formula: 𝐷∇ 2 𝑓 𝑠 = -2i𝐸 𝑓 𝑠 + i 𝛼 4𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑑 𝑧 (8a) 𝐷∇ 2 𝑑 𝑧 = -2i𝐸 𝑑 𝑧 + i 𝛼 4𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑓 𝑠 .(8b)
div.p: Interestingly, in contrast to conventional diffusive antiferromagnetic metals [49][50][51], altermagnets exhibit singlet-triplet conversion also in the diffusive limit. However, from the length scale analysis, we expect the effect to be smaller than in the clean limit [27,28]. We find that the altermagnet has three principal effects on the superconducting correlations. The effects depend strongly on the orientation of the altermagnet because of the 𝑑-wave character of the electronic spin-splitting. These effects can be experimentally observed in both the local density of states and the magnetization of the system. To compute these quantities in an unambiguous way within quasiclassical theory, we consider a normal metal coupled to the upper or lower edge of the altermagnet via a tunneling interface. Fig. 2(a) shows the experimental setup. In the normal metal, ǧ2 = 1 holds when it is in contact with a normal reservoir (see Supp. Mat. for technical details). The proximity-induced magnetization is then computed from
div.formula: 𝑀 𝑧 = 𝑀 0 Δ -1 0 ∫ 𝑑𝐸Tr τ3 ĝ𝐾 /8.
div.p: Here, 𝑀 0 = 𝑔𝜇 𝐵 𝑁 0 Δ 0 and the density of states is 𝑁 = 𝑁 0 Re(Tr{ ĝ𝑅 })/2 with 𝑁 0 being the normal-state value. Moreover, Δ 0 is the superconducting gap in the bulk superconductor. We set the ratio between the normal metal-altermagnet interface conductivity 𝐺 𝑁 0 and the bulk conductivity in the normal metal 𝐺 𝑁 to be
div.formula: 𝐺 𝑁 0 /𝐺 𝑁 𝐿 𝑁 = 0.2𝜉 -1 ,
div.p: where 𝐿 N is the length of the normal metal and 𝜉 = √︁ 𝐷/Δ 0 is the diffusive coherence length. We fix the altermagnetic strength 𝛼 = -0.3.

Supercurrent-induced spin-splitter effect.-First, we consider an altermagnet oriented with 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 in a Josephson junction between two conventional BCS superconductors, such as Nb or Al. This setup is shown in Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 2(a). The KL boundary conditions consistent with the altermagnetic current operator in Eq. ( 6) induce singlet-triplet mixing across the SC-AM interfaces. The resulting triplet correlations 𝑑 𝑧 localize at the edges, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Applying a supercurrent to the system induces a magnetization which is opposite at the upper and lower edge of the altermagnet. This can be interpreted as a dissipationless version of the spin-splitter effect [23]. We therefore call this the supercurrent spin-splitter effect. The resulting magnetization 𝑀 𝑧 in the normal metals is shown in Fig. 2(c) as a function of normal metal position along the altermagnetic edge. The figure shows that the magnetization is almost constant with respect to the normal metal position. However, it has an opposite sign for the two edges, consistent with the mirror symmetry of the system. Notably, the magnetization reverses with the supercurrent, as shown in Fig. 2(d), and vanishes in the absence of a supercurrent. In total, the SC-AM-SC junction provides a controllable supercurrent spin-splitter effect evident from an accumulation of opposite magnetizations at the edges.

Cooper pair spin-splitter effect.-A similar effect arises for Cooper pairs that are spin-polarized along the Néel vector. We again consider an altermagnet with orientation 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 to show this. See Fig. 1(b). To generate triplet superconducting correlations 𝑑 𝑥 , we couple a conventional superconductor, like Nb or Al, to an altermagnet through a very thin ferromagnetic layer, like Co or Ni, with magnetization oriented in the 𝑥direction. The magnetic interface induces a finite 𝑑 𝑥 component in the altermagnet. This effect is captured by the 𝐺 𝜙 term in Eq. (5).

As shown in Eqs. (7), the Cooper pairs split and localize at opposite edges. Fig. 3(a) shows this effect for the spin-up amplitude. Spin-polarized Cooper pairs are generally challenging to detect experimentally [52]. However, the Cooper pair spin-splitter effect gives rise to an associated magnetization in the 𝑦-direction. This can be measured in normal metals connected to the altermagnet as for the supercurrent spin-splitter effect. Fig. 3(b) shows the magnetization in the normal metals connected to the upper or lower edge of the altermagnet.

Cooper pair spin-filtering effect.-We now consider an altermagnet oriented according to 𝜃 = 0 in proximity to an 𝑠-wave superconductor with a thin ferromagnetic interface as shown in Fig. 1 (c). The magnetization of the ferromagnet is perpendicular to the Néel vector of the altermagnet. The primary impact of the altermagnet is spin-filtering of the 𝑓 ↑ and 𝑓 ↓ -correlations. This effect has two contributions. The boundary condition in Eq. ( 5) yields spin-dependent tunneling that effectively spin-filters the spin-polarized Cooper pairs. Hence, the altermagnet gives rise to a spin-selective interfacial transmission as shown in Fig. 4. Furthermore, the altermagnetic

div.head: Mz/M0
div.p: Upper Lower contribution to the Usadel equation (7) gives rise to spindependent decay lengths of the spin-polarized Cooper pairs. As a consequence, the altermagnet serves as an efficient Cooper pair spin filter, as shown in Fig. 4. Finally, we note that upon the completion of this manuscript, a preprint [53] appeared studying a similar setup as in our Fig. 1(a). Concluding remarks.-We have developed a quasiclassical theory for proximity-coupled altermagnets and superconductors. Our theory shows that altermagnets enable the spin degree of freedom in Cooper pairs to be spatially controlled, even in the limit of strong disorder. We find that the altermagnet can control superconducting correlations in three ways. First, we find that the altermagnet can split a supercurrent to induce a finite magnetization at the edges of the altermagnet. The direction of the magnetization is opposite at the two edges. Moreover, the supercurrent controls the magnitude and direction of the magnetization. We call this effect a supercurrent-induced spin-splitter effect. Second, we find that triplet Cooper pairs polarized along the direction of staggered spin order are spatially separated by the altermagnet. In other words, the altermagnet serves as a mediator for creating fully spin-polarized Cooper pairs of opposite spin at opposing edges. We call this the Cooper pair spin-splitter effect. As a third effect, we find that the altermagnet can serve as a filter for spin-polarized Cooper pairs. All these effects can be observed experimentally by either a local magnetization measurement or spin-polarized STM. In total, we find that altermagnets enable dissipationless magnetoelectric effects in materials void of magnetic stray fields.

This work was supported by the Research Council of Norway through Grant No. 323766 and its Centres of Excellence funding scheme Grant No. 262633 "QuSpin." Resources provided by Sigma2-the National Infrastructure for High Performance Computing and Data Storage in Norway, project NN9577K, are gratefully acknowledged.

div.p: Inserting this into Eq. ( 18), we find three terms :
div.formula: - 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 m, Ǧ (R, p, 𝐸) -+ i𝛼ℏ 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 m, Ǧ (R, p, 𝐸) + + 𝛼ℏ 2 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 m, Ǧ (R, p, 𝐸) -,(21)

Furthermore, we used the fact that 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 = 𝑇 𝑗𝑖 to simplify the second term in Eq. ( 21). Thus, we have found a (Gorkov) equation of motion for Green's functions (disregarding the arguments for notional convenience):

div.formula: 𝑚 p • ∇ R Ǧ + [𝐸 ρ3 -Σtot ⊗ , Ǧ] -- 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 m, Ǧ -+ i𝛼ℏ 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 m, Ǧ + + 𝛼ℏ 2 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 ℏ 2 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 m, Ǧ -= 0 ,(22)
div.p: where the energy term comes from the time derivatives in Eq. ( 13), and the total self-energy Σtot contains all other self-energies in the system than altermagnetism. Here, we have introduced the Moyal product 𝐴 ⊗ 𝐵, which is discussed in detail in the next section. The factors of ℏ give all quantities the dimension of energy. We set ℏ = 1 from here.

div.head: C. Length scale analysis
div.p: In this subsection, we discuss the length scales of our system and argue why some terms in Eq. ( 22) can be neglected within the quasiclassical approximation.

Let 𝐿 denote the characteristic length scale for spatial variations, that is, ∇ R Ǧ ∼ 1/𝐿. In the quasiclassical approximation, the relevant momenta are close to the Fermi momentum, i.e. |p| ∼ 1/𝜆 𝐹 , and the length scales 𝜆 𝐹 associated with the momentum are much smaller than all other length scales. Considering the three altermagnetic terms from Eq. ( 22), we see that the first term is of order 1/𝜆 2 𝐹 , the second of order 1/𝜆 𝐹 𝐿, and the third term of order 1/𝐿 2 , which means that the third term should be much smaller thatn the other terms, however, we include also this term in the following to see its effects.

div.head: D. Eilenberger equation
div.p: We now apply the gradient approximation outlined above and introduce the quasiclassical Green's function into Eq. ( 22). We consider the stationary case, where the circle product reduces to matrix multiplication, i.e., 𝐴 • 𝐵 = 𝐴𝐵. We truncate the gradients as explained in Ref. [1] and introduce the quasiclassical Green's function similarly as usual by defining the quantity
The quasiclassical Green's function is sharply peaked at the Fermi surface and is formally defined as
div.formula: ǧ(R, 𝑇, p 𝐹 , 𝐸) = i 𝜋 ∫ 𝑑𝜉 p Ǧ (R, 𝑇, p, 𝐸). (24
div.p: We proceed by multiplying the Gorkov equation in Eq. ( 22) with i/𝜋 and integrate over 𝜉 𝑝 term by term:
div.formula: i 𝜋 ∫ 𝑑𝜉 p 𝑖 𝑚 p • ∇ R Ǧ = i 𝑚 p 𝐹 • ∇ R ǧ, (25
) - i 𝜋 𝛼 2𝑚 ∫ 𝑑𝜉 p 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 m, Ǧ -= - 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝑝 𝐹, 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] -,(26)
div.p: i
div.formula: 𝜋 i𝛼 2𝑚 ∫ 𝑑𝜉 p 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 m, Ǧ + = i𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] + (27
) i 𝜋 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 ∫ 𝑑𝜉 p m, Ǧ -= 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] -.(28)
div.p: Thus, we have found the Eilenberger equation, including three altermagnetic terms:
div.formula: i 𝑚 p 𝐹 • ∇ R ǧ + [𝐸 ρ3 -Σtot , ǧ] -- 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝑝 𝐹, 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] -+ i𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝜕 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] + + 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑖 𝜕 𝑗 [ m, ǧ] -= 0. (29
div.p: The last two terms in Eq. ( 29) are incompatible with the usual normalization condition ǧ2 = 1 [2].

div.head: II. DERIVATION OF THE LINEARIZED USADEL EQUATION
div.p: In the following, we proceed by linearizing the Eilenberger equation ( 29) before taking the dirty limit, averaging the Eilenberger equation over the Fermi surface. This allows us to derive a linearized Usadel equation without a normalization condition like that in Ref. [2].

div.head: A. Linearization of the Eilenberger equation
div.p: We consider the retarded component of Eq. ( 29), and insert the linearization condition ĝ𝑅 = ρ3 + f , where f by assumption is a small quantity, which in general includes both diagonal and off-diagonal parts.
Due to the general symmetries of the quasiclassical Green's function, f reads
div.formula: f = 𝑓 ¯𝑑 𝑓 ¯𝑜 - f ¯𝑜 - f ¯𝑑 ,(30)
div.p: where the tilde operation is a composite energy inversion and complex conjugation, i.e., Ã(𝐸) = 𝐴 * (-𝐸). Moreover, we keep only the self-energy Σtot , and consider the upper right and the upper left 2 × 2 parts of Eq. ( 29) separately. Crucially, the energy term only contributes to the off-diagonal part of the equations since the diagonal part drops out of the commutator
We find two coupled differential equations for the 2 × 2 matrices 𝑓 ¯𝑜 and 𝑓 ¯𝑑: i
div.formula: 𝑚 p 𝐹 • ∇ R 𝑓 ¯𝑜 + 2𝐸 𝑓 ¯𝑜 -[ Σ𝑅 imp , ρ3 + f ] u.r. -- 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝑝 𝐹, 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑜 - + i𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑜 + + 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑜 - = 0 (32a) i 𝑚 p 𝐹 • ∇ R 𝑓 ¯𝑑 -[ Σ𝑅 imp , ρ3 + f ] u.l. -- 𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝑝 𝐹, 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑑 - + i𝛼 2𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝐹,𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑑 + + 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑑 - = 0,(32b)
div.p: where the "u.r(l)." superscript implies the upper right (left) 2 × 2 part of the matrix. In the next section, we argue that we can extract observables from only the off-diagonal equation (32a).

div.head: B. The diagonal contribution
div.p: This section proposes a setup that renders the diagonal corrections 𝑓 𝑑 negligible. The diagonal component 𝑓 𝑑 arises from the lack of a normalization condition for the quasiclassical Green's function in the altermagnet. However, suppose we consider normal metals connected to the altermagnet, as shown in the main text in Fig. 2(a). In that case, we can derive observable quantities from the normalization condition in the normal metal. We also find that 𝑓 𝑑 can be disregarded in these expressions.

In the following, we compute Green's function in the normal metal due to its proximity to the altermagnet, which hosts superconducting correlations originating from the superconductor. Thus, it has a normal and anomalous correction to its Green's function matrix.

The anomalous Green's function in the altermagnet is known from solving Eq. (32a). However, the diagonal part (normal part) of the Green's function is unknown. We write the diffusive, retarded Green's function in the altermagnet as

which is a general expression of the symmetries of the quasiclassical Green's function. We emphasize that ( ĝ𝐴 ) 2 and 𝐷 ¯are unknown in the altermagnet, but 𝐴 ¯is known. Next, we consider the normal metal Green's function. For a weak proximity effect (due to the tunnel coupling to the AM), we can write it as
where f must be off-diagonal due to the fact that ( ĝ𝑁 ) 2 = 1 in the normal metal. As a consequence, we have

We will now show that we can determine f in the normal metal from the quantity 𝐴 ¯in the altermagnet alone, allowing us to compute the density of states and magnetization in the normal metal. To do this, we consider the tunnel boundary condition between N and AM, which is given by the KL boundary condition:
div.formula: 2𝐺 𝑁 𝐿 𝑁 ĝ𝑁 𝜕 𝑥 ĝ𝑁 = 𝐺 0 [ ĝ𝐴 , ĝ𝑁 ],(36)
div.p: where 𝐺 𝑁 is the normal metal bulk conductance and 𝐺 0 is the interfacial conductance. We proceed by inserting ĝ𝐴 and ĝ𝑁 above. We get for the upper right block of this matrix equation that

The terms 𝐴 ¯, 𝐷 ¯, and 𝑓 ¯are small. They are corrections in the linearization procedure. In effect, 𝐷 ¯𝑓 ¯should be negligible compared to 𝐴 ¯and 𝑓 ¯alone because it is second order in small quantities. Here, 𝐷 ¯is the correction to ρ3 for the diagonal part of the Green's function in the altermagnet. It should be small in the limit of weak altermagnetism, and 𝑓 is the correction to the anomalous Green's function in the normal metal, which is also small since it is tunnel coupled to the AM. In contrast, 𝐴 is a first-order correction to the off-diagonal part of the Green's function in the altermagnet due to its proximity to the superconductor. Thus, we approximate Eq. ( 37) as
Consequently, the anomalous Green's function in an N tunnel coupled to the AM is independent of the diagonal corrections 𝐷 ¯to the Green's function in the AM.

div.head: Derivation of the amplitude in the normal metals
div.p: In the limit of a semi-infinite normal metal, the solution for the anomalous Green's function is
div.formula: 𝑓 ¯𝑁 = 𝐶 ¯𝑒-𝑘𝑦 .(39)
div.p: More specifically, we assume that the normal metal is longer than the diffusive coherence length and that the interface normal is parallel to the 𝑦 direction. Here, 𝑘 = √︁ -2i𝐸/𝐷 describes the decay rate of the solution. The KL boundary condition in the normal metal is given in Eq. (38), and becomes
div.formula: 𝐺 𝑁 𝐿 𝑁 𝜕 𝑦 𝑓 ¯𝑁 = -𝐺 0 ( 𝑓 ¯𝑁 + 𝑓 ¯𝐴𝑀 ).(40)
div.p: We set the factor 𝐺 0 /(𝐺 𝑁 𝐿 𝑁 ) ≡ Ω = 0.2𝜉 -1 , and insert the solution from Eq. ( 39) to find
div.formula: -𝑘𝐶𝑒 -𝑘 𝑦 = -Ω(𝐶 ¯𝑒-𝑘𝑦 + 𝑓 ¯𝐴𝑀 ),(41)
div.p: which we can solve for 𝐶 at 𝑦 = 0:
div.formula: 𝐶 ¯= Ω 𝑓 ¯𝐴𝑀 𝑘 -Ω .(42)
div.p: In the next section, we take the diffusive limit and derive the Usadel equation from the linearized Eilenberger. Since we can disregard diagonal corrections in our calculation of observable quantities, we drop the "𝑜" subscript in the following.

div.head: C. Dirty limit and expansion of the quasiclassical Green's function
div.p: Defining the direction of the Fermi momentum as e 𝐹 = p 𝐹 /|p 𝐹 |, we expand the (linearized) quasiclassical Green's function to linear order in spherical harmonics,

assuming that the strong impurity scattering causes the 𝑠-wave part to be much larger than the 𝑝-wave part, 𝑓 ¯𝑠 ≫ 𝒇 ¯𝑝. This is the so-called dirty limit, corresponding to quasiparticle diffusion. In this expansion, the first term is even in momentum, whereas the second is odd. We note that we can isolate the even part of the Green's function by averaging over the (solid angle of the) Fermi surface,
div.formula: ∫ 𝑑e 𝐹 2𝜋 𝑓 ¯= 𝑓 ¯𝑠,(44)
div.p: similarly, we can isolate the odd part by first multiplying with e 𝐹 and then performing the average
div.formula: ∫ 𝑑e 𝐹 2𝜋 e 𝐹 𝑓 ¯= 1 2 𝒇 ¯𝑝.(45)
div.p: In the following, we split Eq. (32a) into an even and an odd equation. Performing the averages for the altermagnetic terms produces:
div.formula: - 𝛼2𝑚
div.p: where we, in the final part of the first equation, used that the 𝑇-matrix from Eq. ( 2) is traceless and introduced the Fermi velocity 𝑣 𝐹 = 𝑝 𝐹 /𝑚. Consequently, the even equation becomes
div.formula: i𝑣 𝐹 2 ∇ R • 𝑓 ¯𝑝 + 2𝐸 𝑓 ¯𝑠 + i𝛼𝑣 𝐹 4 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑝,𝑖 + + 𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯𝑠 - = 0. (49a
div.p: We now assume that impurity scattering is the dominating term such that the odd altermagnetic terms are negligible. Using Σimp = -i ǧ𝑠 /(2𝜏) for the impurity self-energy [1], where 𝜏 is the elastic scattering time, we find
div.formula: -[ Σimp , ĝ𝑅 ] = i 2𝜏 [ ĝ𝑅 𝑠 , ĝ𝑅 ] -= i 2𝜏 [ ρ3 + f𝑠 , ρ3 + f𝑠 + e 𝐹 • f𝑝 ] ≈ i 2𝜏 [ ρ3 , e 𝐹 • f𝑝 ](50)
= i 2𝜏 e 𝐹 • [ ρ3 , f𝑝 ] -= i 2𝜏 e 𝐹 • 0 ¯𝒇 ¯𝑝 f ¯𝑝 0 ¯ .(51)
div.p: We multiply Eq. ( 32b) with e 𝐹 and average over the Fermi surface to find the odd equation:
div.formula: 𝒇 𝑝 = -𝜏𝑣 𝐹 ∇ R 𝑓 ¯𝑠 (52
div.p: Inserting Eq. ( 52) into Eq. ( 49) and multiplying with i, we find the Usadel equation as two decoupled equations for the 2 × 2 matrix 𝑓 ¯:
div.formula: 𝐷∇ 2 R 𝑓 ¯= -2i𝐸 𝑓 ¯-𝛼𝐷 2 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]+ - i𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]-,(53)
div.p: where we defined the diffusion constant 𝐷 = 𝜏𝑣 2 𝐹 /2. The factor 2 in the denominator of the diffusion constant is because this is a 2D system. More generally, the diffusion constant reads 𝐷 = 𝜏𝑣 2 𝐹 /𝑑, where 𝑑 is the spatial dimension of the system.

div.head: D. Singlet-triplet decomposition
div.p: We follow Ref. [3] and introduce a singlet/triplet decomposition,
div.formula: 𝑓 ¯= ( 𝑓 𝑠 + d • 𝝈)i𝜎 ¯2 = i𝑑 𝑦 -𝑑 𝑥 𝑑 𝑧 + 𝑓 𝑠 𝑑 𝑧 -𝑓 𝑠 i𝑑 𝑦 + 𝑑 𝑥 ,(54)
div.p: where 𝑓 𝑠 denotes the superconducting singlet amplitude and d the superconducting triplet amplitudes. We calculate the second term on the right-hand side of Eq. ( 53) using this decomposition and find
div.formula: 𝛼𝐷 2 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]+ = 𝛼𝐷𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑖𝑑 𝑦 -𝑑𝑥 0 0 -𝑖𝑑 𝑦 -𝑑 𝑥 .(55)
div.p: The third term takes the form
div.formula: i𝛼 8𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 [𝜏 ¯3, 𝑓 ¯]-= i𝛼 4𝑚 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 0 𝑑 𝑧 + 𝑓 𝑠 𝑓 𝑠 -𝑑 𝑧 0 .(56)

div.head: B. Modifying the Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions
div.p: The spin-active Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions in quasiclassical theory are expressed as [5,6] 2
div.formula: Ǐ𝑅 = 𝐺 0 [ ǧ𝐿 , ǧ𝑅 ] -+ i𝐺 𝜙 [ ǧ𝑅 , m𝑥 ] -,(65)
div.p: where the matrix current can be expressed in terms of the right-hand side material
div.formula: Ǐ𝑅 = 𝐺 𝑅 𝐿 𝑅 ǧ𝑅 (n • ∇ R ) ǧ𝑅 ,(66)
div.p: where ǧ𝐿(𝑅) is the quasiclassical Green's function to the left (right), and we have included a possible spin polarization in the 𝑥-direction. In Eq. (66), 𝐺 𝐿 is the bulk conductance of material 𝐿, and 𝐿 𝐿 is the length of the material. For the interface where the AM is on the left side, the boundary condition is obtained by letting Ǐ𝑅 → -Ǐ𝐿 on the left-hand side and ǧ𝐿 ↔ ǧ𝑅 on the right-hand side of the boundary condition equation. In the following, we denote the conductance of the altermagnet as 𝐺 and its length as 𝐿. 𝐺 0 denotes the interface conductance, and 𝐺 𝜙 is the (spin-mixing) conductance of the magnetic term. We have assumed the interface polarization to be in the 𝑥-direction. The Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions are derived using the quasiclassical current operator and are modified under the presence of altermagnetism in, e.g., the right region:
div.formula: 2𝐺 𝐿 ǧ𝑅 (∇ 𝑅 𝑖 + 𝛼𝜎 ¯𝑧𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 ) ǧ𝑅 = 𝐺 0 [ ǧ𝐿 , ǧ𝑅 ] -+ i𝐺 𝜙 [ ǧ𝑅 , m𝑥 ] -,(67)
div.p: where the repeated Latin indices imply the sum over the Cartesian components. The corresponding equation for the Green's function on the right side of the interface follows from a substitution n → -n and ǧ𝐿 ↔ ǧ𝑅 , as well as a modification of the current operator in the altermagnet due to the velocity operator being different from a normal metal.

div.head: C. Linearization
div.p: We linearize the (retarded component of the) Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions in Eq. (65), i.e. ĝ𝑅 = ρ3 + f𝑅 , and include terms O( f𝑅 ) on the left-hand side, and terms O( f 0 𝑅 ) on the right-hand side:
div.formula: 2𝐺 𝐿 ρ3 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 + 𝛼𝜎 ¯𝑧𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 f𝑅 = 𝐺 0 [ ĝ𝐿 , ρ3 ] + i𝐺 𝜙 [ f𝑅 , m𝑥 ],(68)
div.p: where f𝐿(𝑅) has the symmetries described in Eq. (30). To see how the boundary condition affects the diagonal part 𝑓 ¯𝑑 and the off-diagonal part 𝑓 ¯𝑜, we multiply Eq. (68) with ρ3 from the left and find:
div.formula: 2𝐺 𝐿𝑛 𝑖 𝜕 𝑖 + 𝛼𝜎 ¯𝑧𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑗 𝑓 ¯𝑑 𝑓 ¯𝑜 -f𝑜 ¯-f𝑑 ¯ 𝑅 = -2𝐺 0 0 𝑓 ¯𝑜 -f𝑜 ¯0 𝐿 + i𝐺 𝜙 [ 𝑓 ¯𝑑, 𝜎 𝑥 ] -[ 𝑓 ¯𝑜, 𝜎 𝑥 ] - [ f𝑜 ¯, 𝜎 𝑥 ] -[ 𝑓 ¯𝑑, 𝜎 𝑥 ] -𝑅 . (69
div.p: The boundary conditions for 𝑓 ¯𝑑 and 𝑓 ¯𝑜 are decoupled to the first order in f . We will not need to consider the diagonal parts in more detail, as described in Sec. II B, and drop the subscript in 𝑓 ¯𝑜 from here. The linearized Kuprianov-Lukichev boundary condition for the anomalous Green's function for any altermagnetic orientation reads
div.formula: 2𝐺 𝐿 𝜕 𝑅 𝑖 + 𝛼𝜎 ¯𝑧𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝜕 𝑅 𝑗 𝑓 ¯𝑅 = -2𝐺 0 𝑓 ¯𝐿 + i𝐺 𝜙 [ 𝑓 ¯𝑅, 𝜎 ¯𝑥] -.(70)
div.p: It is instructive to consider some special cases of this. We consider an interface along the 𝑥-direction and the 𝜃 = 0 and 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 orientations of the altermagnet separately. We assume an 𝑠-wave singlet BCS superconductor in 𝐿, i.e.
div.formula: 𝑓 ¯𝐿 = 0 𝑓 BCS -𝑓 BCS 0 ,(71)
div.p: where 𝑓 BCS is the (bulk) value of the anomalous Green's function in the superconductor. Moreover, the spin-active term becomes
div.formula: [ 𝑓 ¯𝑅, 𝜎 ¯𝑥] -= 2 𝑓 𝑠 -𝑑 𝑥 𝑑 𝑥 -𝑓 𝑠 𝑅 . (72
div.p: We detail the two special cases for the altermagnetic orientations in the following.
for the singlet/triplet amplitudes 𝑓 1/2 = 𝑓 𝑠 ± 𝑑 𝑧 . These equations show that the singlet and triplet amplitudes mix at the boundary and that the mixing scales with the altermagnetic strength 𝛼.
for the singlet/triplet amplitudes 𝑓 1/2 = 𝑓 𝑠 ± 𝑑 𝑧 .

div.head: IV. SOLUTION STRATEGY
div.p: This section outlines and summarizes the numerical solution strategy used to solve the derived Usadel equations with the corresponding Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions.

We solve for the singlet/triplet amplitudes in the normal metals connected to the altermagnet, as described in Sec. II B, and use these to obtain observable quantities such as magnetization. To do this, we solve the Usadel equations ( 59) and (60) together with the boundary conditions (70) to determine the amplitude in the altermagnet. The four different Cooper pair anomalous Green's function amplitudes are coupled. However, the equal-spin triplet amplitude 𝑑 𝑥 is negligible in the weak proximity regime. We solve for 𝑓 1,2 and use the result in the boundary conditions for the equal-spin triplets. n this limit, the equations for 𝑓 1,2 and 𝑓 ↑,↓ decouple. In this paper, we solve these equations using a simple finite difference scheme on a square geometry.
Finally, we use the amplitudes in the altermagnet to find the amplitudes in the normal metal using Eq. ( 42). This allows us to compute the magnetization in the normal metal.

figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. (Color online) (a) A Josephson junction consisting of two conventional BCS superconductors separated by an altermagnet. A supercurrent induces an edge magnetization 𝑀 𝑧 due to the superconducting proximity effect. The sign of the magnetization is controllable by the applied supercurrent. (b) Spin-polarized Cooper pairs spatially separate in an altermagnet and localize at opposite edges. (c) An altermagnet filters spin-polarized Cooper pairs. The decay length discrepancy between the ↑↑ and ↓↓ Cooper pairs is determined by the altermagnetic strength and orientation.

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. (Color online) (a) Proposed experimental setup for measuring the supercurrent spin-splitter effect: attaching a normal electrode to the AM edge allows the magnetization to be measured via a spindependent probe such as nano-SQUID or spin-polarized STM. (b) The real part of the triplet correlations 𝑑 𝑧 for a finite phase difference Δ𝜙 = 𝜋/2 and energy 𝐸 = Δ 0 /2. (c) The magnetization 𝑀 𝑧 and the induced by the supercurrent for a phase difference Δ𝜙 = 𝜋/2 in a normal metal probe connected at position 𝑥 along the upper and lower edges of the altermagnet in units of the diffusive coherence length 𝜉 = √︁ 𝐷/Δ 0 . The inverse temperature 𝛽 = 16Δ -1 0 . (d) The magnetization 𝑀 𝑧 in the normal metal at the middle position of the upper edge in the altermagnet as a function of the phase difference Δ𝜙 for three different inverse temperatures.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. (Color online) (a) The magnitude of the spin-up amplitude 𝑓 ↑ in the AM as a function of position, given by the coordinates 𝑥 and 𝑦. (b) The magnetization 𝑀 𝑦 in a normal metal that is connected at position 𝑥 along the upper or lower edge of the altermagnet for an inverse temperature 𝛽 = 16Δ -1 0 .

div.head: Supplementary material for Quasiclassical theory of Cooper pair spin-splitter and filtering effect via altermagnets
div.p: Hans Gløckner Giil, Bjørnulf Brekke, Jacob Linder, and Arne Brataas
Center for Quantum Spintronics, Department of Physics, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, NO-7491 Trondheim, Norway

div.head: I. DERIVATION OF THE EILENBERGER EQUATION IN AN ALTERMAGNET
A. The Hamiltonian
div.p: We describe altermagnetism by the effective Hamiltonian,

where 𝛼 is the dimensionless altermagnetic strength, l is the Néel vector orientation, and 𝑝 𝑖 = -iℏ∇ 𝑖 denotes the Cartesian component 𝑖 of the canonical momentum. In Eq. ( 1) and below, we use the Einstein summation convention for Cartesian components, i.e., 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 = 𝑖, 𝑗 𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑖 𝑝 𝑗 . The matrix 𝑇 determines the real space orientation of the altermagnet. We parametrize this orientation by the angle 𝜃 as

In the main text, we consider the special cases of 𝜃 = 0 and 𝜃 = 𝜋/4 in detail. These cases correspond to the terms 𝑝 2 𝑥 -𝑝 2 𝑦 and 2𝑝 𝑥 𝑝 𝑦 in the Hamiltonian 𝐻 𝐴𝑀 , respectively. In the following, we consider the contribution from the Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 1) to the quasiclassical equations of motion, following the methodology of Ref. [1].

div.head: B. Heisenberg equations of motion
div.p: In this section, we derive equations of motion for the field operators 𝜓 𝜎 and 𝜓 † 𝜎 . These lead to equations of motion for the Green's functions. The Heisenberg equation of motion for the field operator 𝜓 𝜎 for a general Hamiltonian 𝐻 reads
where we denote (anti)commutators by [ 𝐴, 𝐵] ± = 𝐴𝐵 ± 𝐵𝐴. We express the commutators as anticommutators by using the identity
and use the equal-time anti-commutation relations of the fermionic field operators,
The Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 1) gives rise to the altermagnetic contribution to the equations of motion
The equations of motion for the adjoint operators are readily obtained by taking the Hermitian conjugate
and we find

It is convenient to introduce the quantities 𝑓 ↑ = i𝑑 𝑦 -𝑑 𝑥 and 𝑓 ↓ = i𝑑 𝑦 + 𝑑 𝑥 , and transform Eq. ( 57) into:

Here, 𝑓 ↑ and 𝑓 ↓ are condensate amplitudes for Cooper pair triplets with spin up and down, respectively. Similarly, it is convenient to introduce 𝑓 1 = 𝑓 𝑠 + 𝑑 𝑧 and 𝑓 2 = 𝑓 𝑠 -𝑑 𝑧 and transform Eq. ( 58):

div.head: III. DERIVATION OF QUASICLASSICAL BOUNDARY CONDITIONS
div.p: In this section, we derive boundary conditions for interfaces with altermagnets using a tunneling Hamiltonian. The derivation is done by adjusting the Kupriyanov-Lukichev boundary conditions, which can be done using the methodology of [4], with a current operator consistent with the altermagnet.

div.head: A. The quasiclassical current operator in an altermagnet
div.p: The effective altermagnetic Hamiltonian is
Hamilton's equation of motion implies that the velocity operator is spin-and momentum-dependent. It is on the form
Here, we have used the chain rule and the symmetry of 𝑇 to write 𝜕 𝑝 𝑖 (𝑇 𝑙 𝑗 𝑝 𝑙 𝑝 𝑗 ) = 2𝑇 𝑖 𝑗 𝑝 𝑗 . For the two special cases 𝜃 = 0 and 𝜃 = 𝜋/4, the velocity operator reads
The quasiclassical current operator should be modified accordingly:

From Eq. (64) and Eq. (63), it is apparent that the boundary conditions are spin-dependent, and couple the 𝑥 and 𝑦 components in the case of 𝜃 = 𝜋/4. The momentum components 𝑝 𝑥 and 𝑝 𝑦 are more generally coupled for any 𝜃 ≠ 0.
